title: Data Visualization with Python
description: Learn data visualization fundamentals using matplotlib and seaborn through interactive modules with real examples and assessments.

nodes.title: Introduction to Matplotlib
nodes.content: Matplotlib is Python's foundational visualization library providing low-level control over plots. It enables creation of line charts, bar plots, and scatter plots through the pyplot interface. Matplotlib works by creating figures and axes, then adding visual elements like lines, markers, and labels. Understanding matplotlib is essential before exploring higher-level libraries like seaborn. Most Python visualizations built today use matplotlib as their underlying engine.

nodes.title: Creating a Simple Line Chart
nodes.content: Create a basic line chart showing temperature across days: import matplotlib.pyplot as plt; plt.figure(figsize=(8, 5)); plt.plot(days, temps, 'b-o'); plt.xlabel('Days'); plt.ylabel('Temperature (°C)'); plt.title('Temperature Trend'); plt.show(). This demonstrates the complete matplotlib workflow: import, create figure, plot data, label axes, and display.

nodes.title: Matplotlib Fundamentals
nodes.content: Test your understanding of matplotlib basics and plotting principles.

questions.question: What is the correct way to set the title of a matplotlib plot?
feedback: Incorrect. plt.set_title() is not a valid method.
Correct! plt.title() is the standard way to set plot titles.
Incorrect. Use plt.title() instead.
Incorrect. plt.plot() doesn't accept title as a parameter.
questions.explanation: plt.title() is the pyplot method for setting plot titles. Remember that plt is a module-level interface to matplotlib.

questions.question: Which library extension provides high-level statistical visualizations built on matplotlib?
feedback: Incorrect. Plotly is a separate library, not built on matplotlib.
Correct! Seaborn builds on matplotlib for statistical visualizations.
Incorrect. Bokeh is for interactive visualization.
Incorrect. Folium is for map visualizations.
questions.explanation: Seaborn is built on top of matplotlib and provides beautiful statistical visualizations with minimal code.

nodes.title: Advanced Visualizations with Seaborn
nodes.content: Seaborn is a high-level Python visualization library that simplifies statistical plotting. Built on matplotlib, it provides functions for creating heatmaps, violin plots, and multi-plot grids with automatic color palettes and styling. Seaborn excels at visualizing relationships across multiple variables and conditional distributions with minimal code. The library integrates seamlessly with pandas DataFrames, enabling intuitive data exploration. Understanding seaborn transforms complex statistical visualizations into simple, publication-quality plots.

nodes.title: Creating a Multi-Plot Dashboard
nodes.content: Create a dashboard with multiple seaborn plots: fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 2, figsize=(12, 10)); sns.scatterplot(data=df, x='age', y='salary', hue='department', ax=axes[0,0]); sns.boxplot(data=df, x='department', y='salary', ax=axes[0,1]); sns.heatmap(corr_matrix, ax=axes[1,0]); sns.histplot(data=df, x='salary', ax=axes[1,1]); plt.tight_layout(). This pattern lets you combine multiple visualization types into one organized dashboard.

nodes.title: Advanced Visualization Techniques
nodes.content: Evaluate your understanding of seaborn and multi-variable visualization strategies.

questions.question: What seaborn parameter allows you to color points by a categorical variable?
feedback: Incorrect. color_by is not a seaborn parameter.
Correct! The 'hue' parameter creates separate colors for each category.
Incorrect. palette controls the color scheme but not categorical grouping.
Incorrect. category is not a seaborn parameter.
questions.explanation: The 'hue' parameter in seaborn functions (scatterplot, lineplot, etc.) creates separate colors for each category in your data.

questions.question: Which seaborn plot type best displays distribution shapes across multiple groups?
feedback: Incorrect. lineplot shows relationships over time.
Incorrect. barplot shows means but not full distributions.
Correct! Violinplot displays complete distribution shapes for each group.
Incorrect. regplot is for regression visualization.
questions.explanation: Violinplots show the complete distribution of values for each group, making them ideal for comparing distribution shapes across categories.

nodes.title: Practice: Python Visualization Exercises
nodes.content: Apply your knowledge through hands-on coding. Create a line plot showing monthly sales trends with custom colors and labels. Build scatter plots using the hue parameter to show product categories. Create a subplot grid (2x2) comparing sales metrics across regions. Write code generating a heatmap showing variable correlations. Try creating a violin plot comparing salary distributions by department. Use pandas DataFrames or create sample data to practice these techniques.

questions.question: What code creates a scatter plot with matplotlib?
options: Both A and B create scatter plots
Neither option creates a scatter plot
feedback: Incomplete. Option B also works.
Incomplete. Option A also works.
Correct! Both methods create scatter plots.
Incorrect. Both methods work.
questions.explanation: Both plt.scatter() and plt.plot(x, y, 'o') create scatter plots in matplotlib, though scatter() is more specialized.

questions.question: How do you set the figure size in matplotlib?
feedback: Incorrect. figsize() is not a standalone function.
Correct! Use figsize parameter in plt.figure().
Incorrect. size() is not the right function.
Incorrect. dimensions() is not a matplotlib function.
questions.explanation: The figsize parameter in plt.figure() sets the figure dimensions as (width, height) in inches.

nodes.title: Continue Your Learning
nodes.content: You've completed core data visualization training! You can finish the course or review advanced seaborn techniques in depth. Choose your next step.

nodes.title: Course Complete!
nodes.content: Congratulations on completing Data Visualization with Python! You've mastered matplotlib fundamentals, created professional seaborn visualizations, and built multi-plot dashboards. You can now tackle real-world data exploration projects and create publication-quality charts. Practice with your own datasets, explore specialized libraries like Plotly for interactivity, and continue building your data visualization skills. Great work!
